title: PM Jan Dhan Yojana
one_liner: A simple bank account for more families so wages and benefits can land safely.
what: A national push to open no-frills bank accounts, with RuPay cards and a path to insurance and small credit for people who were outside banking.
why: Many households had no account, so cash was risky and government help was harder to send in a clean, trackable way.
example: Your cousin opens an account at a camp; a scholarship later credits straight in instead of a long queue for cash.
pros: Safer than keeping large cash at home
cons: Some accounts stay unused without follow-up
before: Cash-only habits; benefits sometimes delayed or harder to prove.
after: Named accounts and cards; many payments can move digitally with a clear trail.

quiz.question: Jan Dhan mainly helps by…
options: Bringing more households into basic banking for transfers and safety

title: GST (One Nation, One Tax)
one_liner: One main nationwide goods and services tax instead of a maze of older state and central taxes.
what: GST is a value-added tax on many goods and services, collected in stages, with rules for invoices and input credits so businesses offset tax on inputs.
why: Old tax layers were confusing for trucks, shops, and small firms moving goods across states.
example: A snack brand ships boxes to three states with clearer rules and digital filings instead of many small permits.
pros: Easier to compare rates in one system
cons: Small firms still face compliance load
Tech glitches hurt if backup help is weak
before: Multiple taxes and checkpoints slowed trade and confused bills.
after: One broad framework with GSTN-style digital rails for many sectors.

quiz.question: GST is best summed up as…
options: A new cricket league for tax officers
A unified goods and services tax system across much of India
A tax only on imported luxury yachts
A ban on all small shops

title: PM Street Vendor’s AtmaNirbhar Nidhi (PM SVANidhi)
one_liner: Tiny loans to help street vendors restock and grow after shocks like COVID-19.
what: Micro-credit for eligible street vendors, often in small tiers, to buy stock, tools, or repair carts—with incentives for digital repayments in some cases.
why: Vendors run on thin cash; a week without sales can mean no fresh stock.
example: A juice stall owner borrows a small amount, buys a better blender, and serves more cups per hour.
cons: Not every vendor is mapped or eligible
Local politics can affect fair access
before: Informal credit at harsh rates after income dips.
after: A structured micro-loan lane with a pathway to better discipline and digital trails.

quiz.question: PM SVANidhi mainly targets…
options: Street vendors needing small working capital loans
Only farm land purchases above ₹1 crore

title: National Education Policy (NEP) 2020
one_liner: A big roadmap: flexible learning, less rote, more skills—implemented step by step by states.
what: A policy guide for school and college change—think 5+3+3+4 stages, more vocational exposure, teacher training, and multidisciplinary options—not one overnight switch.
why: Old tracks felt rigid early; employers wanted thinking and teamwork, not only long memorised answers.
example: A school adds a short project week: climate poster + basic data, presented like a mini science fair.
pros: Room for projects and critical thinking
Vocational paths gain respect on paper
Early childhood gets more focus in design
cons: Needs money, teachers, and time in each state
Exam culture will not vanish instantly
before: Heavy exam pressure; lanes like science/commerce/arts felt locked too early for some.
after: Policy nudges flexibility, credits, and skills—but real class vibes still depend on local rollout.

quiz.question: NEP 2020 is mainly…
options: A single app that deletes homework
A national education reform roadmap states adapt over time
A rule banning all board exams forever from day one
A scheme only for foreign students

title: PM POSHAN (Mid-Day Meal)
one_liner: Free hot lunch at school so hunger doesn’t pull kids out of class.
what: A school meal programme (now under PM POSHAN branding in policy talk) that funds grains, cooking costs, and better kitchens in many government and aided schools.
why: Poor nutrition and empty stomachs were linked to dropout, especially for girls and younger kids.
example: Arjun actually stays till 3 pm because dal-rice is reliable—and he jokes less about ‘lunch money stress’.
pros: Simple idea with huge daily reach
cons: Quality varies with kitchens and hygiene
Leakage or delays in some blocks
before: Some kids skipped school when meals at home were uncertain.
after: A predictable school meal becomes a social anchor and nutrition safety net.

quiz.question: Mid-day meals at school mainly aim to…
options: Improve nutrition and attendance by serving food in school hours

title: Samagra Shiksha
one_liner: One merged umbrella fund for school quality—from foundational literacy to labs and inclusion.
what: Samagra Shiksha bundles many school grants: early learning, RMSA-style secondary support, teacher training, ICT, inclusive education for children with disabilities, and more.
why: Silos made planning messy; states wanted flexible money tied to learning outcomes, not only labels.
example: A panchayat school gets a few new computers and a ramp—same scheme window, less form jungle.
pros: Focus on learning levels, not only buildings
Inclusion budgets in the same frame
before: Several parallel schemes with different forms and timelines.
after: One integrated school education programme with clearer combined planning.

quiz.question: Samagra Shiksha is mainly about…
options: Funding and improving school education holistically
Paying students cash for likes on social media
Closing all private schools in a week

title: Ayushman Bharat (AB-PMJAY)
one_liner: Hospital cost help for many eligible families when a serious illness hits.
what: A health-assurance style package for listed secondary and tertiary care at empanelled hospitals, with an e-card and annual cover limits for eligible families.
why: One big bill could wipe savings; many had no private insurance buffer.
example: A surgery bill drops sharply at an empanelled hospital because the package is covered for that case.
pros: Reduces shock bills for many eligible cases
cons: Not every disease or hospital is covered
before: Borrowing or skipping care after major hospital stays.
after: Eligible families carry an e-card and clearer package cover in many situations.

quiz.question: AB-PMJAY is best described as…
options: Cover for many serious hospital treatments for eligible families at empanelled hospitals
A ban on all private doctors

title: Mission Indradhanush
one_liner: Catch-up drives so babies get life-saving vaccines on time, not months late.
what: Special immunisation rounds in high-focus districts and outreach to reach kids who missed routine vaccine dates.
why: Dropouts in the vaccine schedule meant preventable diseases could still break out.
example: ASHA workers map a slum lane; three infants get their missed pentavalent shots the same week.
cons: Cold chain breaks in heat waves
Needs steady routine services after drives
before: Some pockets had low full-immunisation coverage year after year.
after: Focused campaigns plus routine sessions lift coverage charts in many areas.

quiz.question: Mission Indradhanush mainly pushes…
options: Vaccine catch-up and full immunisation coverage
Free gym memberships for adults only

title: Tele Mental Health Programme
one_liner: Remote counselling so teens and adults can reach help without travel stigma.
what: A network of tele-mental health centres (linked with institutes like NIMHANS in design) to offer phone or video support, referrals, and training for frontline staff.
why: Mental health care was uneven; distance and shame blocked many from walking into a clinic.
example: A college student books a call after panic attacks—first step without skipping class for a faraway city trip.
pros: Lower barrier than only in-person visits
Good for mild to moderate support tiers
cons: Not a substitute for emergency in-person care
Privacy depends on call quality and data rules
Burnout risk for thin helpline teams
before: Long silent struggles; few affordable therapists nearby.
after: A documented tele-ladder for talk support, follow-ups, and referrals when needed.

quiz.question: Tele mental health aims to…
options: Make counselling and support easier to access from home or nearby centres
Give free cars to every student

title: PM-KISAN
one_liner: Small cash instalments for eligible farmer families between tough crop seasons.
what: Direct benefit transfers in instalments to landholding farmer families who pass eligibility checks, usually to a linked bank account.
why: Farm income jumps with rain and prices; many small holders had no cushion for inputs or fees.
example: Grandpa buys fertiliser on time because an instalment arrived before sowing week.
pros: Simple cash design when DBT works
Pushes updated land and bank records
cons: Not a fix for bad prices or drought alone
before: Lean months pushed some toward informal high-interest loans.
after: Small, scheduled transfers add breathing room for many eligible families.

quiz.question: PM-KISAN mainly gives…
options: Instalments of income support to eligible landholding farmer families

title: Soil Health Card Scheme
one_liner: Soil tests turned into a simple card so farmers know what the land actually needs.
what: Field sampling and lab tests summarised on a card: nutrient status and fertiliser dose hints crop-wise, in farmer-friendly language.
why: Blind fertiliser use wasted money and hurt soil over time.
example: A wheat farmer cuts urea a bit after the card shows enough nitrogen—saves cash and runoff.
pros: Teaches soil as a balance sheet
Cheap at scale compared to yield loss
before: Guesswork and neighbour-copy habits on fertiliser bags.
after: Printed/digital hints tied to tested plots in many districts.

quiz.question: Soil Health Cards mainly help farmers…
options: Understand soil nutrients and apply fertiliser more wisely

title: eNAM (National Agriculture Market)
one_liner: Online mandi trading links so farmers can see more buyers and less middleman drama.
what: A digital trading platform connecting Agricultural Produce Market Committees (APMCs) for listed commodities—price discovery, bids, and smoother logistics in intent.
why: Local cartels sometimes crushed farm gate prices; transparent bids were rare.
example: A tomato grower checks live bids from two linked mandis before loading the truck route.
pros: Digital record of trades when used honestly
cons: Not all markets are equally active
before: Seller hears only one yard’s whisper rate.
after: More yards on one digital grid for listed crops in participating states.

quiz.question: eNAM is mainly about…
options: National online linkage of mandis for better price discovery

title: MGNREGA
one_liner: Up to 100 days of paid rural work on local projects when families ask for it.
what: A legal guarantee of wage work on approved unskilled tasks—ponds, paths, soil work—with job cards and muster rolls for transparency.
why: Lean farm seasons left families with zero cash and risky migration choices.
example: Monsoon fails; a village opens MGNREGA weeks on a bund—wages buy rice till the next crop.
pros: Women’s share is strong on paper
cons: Urban poor are outside the guarantee
before: No statutory fallback when farm work vanished overnight.
after: Demand-led workdays with wage lists—imperfect but real in many panchayats.

quiz.question: MGNREGA guarantees up to…
options: 100 days of wage work a financial year for eligible rural households

title: Skill India Mission
one_liner: Training and standards so young people leave courses with skills employers recognise.
what: A umbrella brand for NSDC partners, PMKVY-style short courses, apprenticeships, and industry-linked certificates—not magic jobs, but clearer skill lanes.
why: Degrees alone didn’t match shop-floor or service needs; youth unemployment stayed sticky.
example: A welder course plus assessment badge helps a leaver join a small fabrication unit faster.
before: Theory-heavy courses with weak lab hours.
after: More competency tests, apprenticeships, and sector councils in the conversation.

quiz.question: Skill India mainly promotes…
options: Job-linked training and recognised vocational skills

title: National Career Service (NCS)
one_liner: A government job-matching portal plus career counselling tools for first-time job seekers.
what: Online registration, vacancy listings, skill courses links, and career centres that try to reduce fraud middlemen in informal hiring.
why: Many youth relied on costly agents for basic job info.
example: A diploma holder uploads a CV template from a cyber café and applies to two local factory openings.
pros: Some states plug local fairs into it
Free core access for job seekers
cons: Not every real job is listed
before: Paper notices on random walls; agent fees for the same news.
after: One searchable lane with verified employer attempts and counselling hooks.

quiz.question: National Career Service is mainly…
options: A portal ecosystem for jobs, skills, and career guidance
A scheme to hire only robots

title: Digital India Programme
one_liner: Better internet, e-gov services, and electronics manufacturing—India online but inclusive.
what: A flagship bundle: broadband reach, cloud gov platforms, cyber awareness, and incentives for electronics assembly and design-linked jobs.
why: Paper queues and poor connectivity wasted time; phones became universal before services caught up.
example: A ration card update starts on a state app; biometric check at the fair price shop finishes the loop.
pros: Shared rails like UPI help daily life
before: Certificates meant multiple office visits and photocopies.
after: Many services have online or hybrid paths with shared digital IDs where allowed.

quiz.question: Digital India is mainly about…
options: Digital infrastructure, e-governance, and inclusive online access

title: DigiLocker
one_liner: Your marksheets and IDs in a secure digital wallet—less Xerox drama.
what: A cloud locker issued by the government where verified issuers push digital documents citizens can share with consent.
why: Colleges, banks, and airlines asked for the same paper again and again.
example: You show a verified class-12 PDF from DigiLocker at admission instead of rushing for attested copies.
pros: Share links with expiry in some flows
Backed by Aadhaar-style KYC where enabled
cons: Not every office accepts e-copies yet
before: Folders of laminated copies for every new form.
after: Signed digital copies in many institutions that trust the issuer.

quiz.question: DigiLocker mainly lets you…
options: Store and share verified digital documents

title: BharatNet
one_liner: Fiber to gram panchayats so villages can study, trade, and telemedicine without crawling speeds.
what: A national optical fibre backbone project to connect local bodies and offer bandwidth partners can light up for Wi-Fi or broadband.
why: Village uploads failed during online exams; tele-health needed stable pipes.
example: A coaching class streams a free YouTube revision session without buffering for the whole group.
pros: Attracts small ISPs where open access works
cons: Power cuts break the dream if not paired
before: Patchy 2G for homework research in many hamlets.
after: Fibre at the panchayat anchor; states and operators fight the last hop.

quiz.question: BharatNet focuses on…
options: Optical fibre broadband to gram panchayats as digital backbone

title: Digital Rupee
one_liner: India created its own official digital currency.
what: A digital version of Indian Rupee launched by RBI.
why: To support faster and secure digital payments.
example: People can use digital rupee on phone instead of cash.

quiz.question: The Digital Rupee is best described as…
options: A private cryptocurrency not tied to the rupee
An RBI-backed digital form of the Indian rupee

title: PM Mudra Yojana
one_liner: Small businesses can get loans more easily.
what: A scheme providing loans to small businesses and startups.
why: Many small shopkeepers and entrepreneurs struggled to get loans.
example: A tea stall owner gets money to expand business.

quiz.question: PM Mudra Yojana mainly helps…
options: Small businesses and entrepreneurs access loans

title: Faceless Income Tax Assessment
one_liner: Income tax process became more digital and transparent.
what: An online tax assessment system without physical meetings.
why: To reduce corruption and make tax process easier.
example: People submit tax details online instead of visiting offices.

quiz.question: Faceless tax assessment means…
options: Assessment happens digitally without needing to meet an officer in person
Tax is decided by random lottery

title: Direct Benefit Transfer
one_liner: Government money now goes directly into bank accounts.
what: A system that transfers subsidies directly to citizens.
why: Middlemen and corruption delayed benefits earlier.
example: Scholarship money directly reaches student account.

quiz.question: DBT mainly does which of these?
options: Sends scheme money straight to eligible people’s bank accounts
Replaces banks with cash vans only

title: Sovereign Gold Bond Scheme
one_liner: People can invest in gold digitally without buying jewellery.
what: Government bonds linked to gold prices.
why: Physical gold has storage and theft risks.
example: A person invests in gold online instead of buying coins.

quiz.question: Sovereign Gold Bonds are…
options: Government securities whose value tracks gold, held digitally
Free gold coins for every voter

title: Atal Pension Yojana
one_liner: Workers can receive pension after retirement.
what: A pension scheme mainly for unorganized workers.
why: Many workers had no financial support in old age.
example: A worker saves monthly and gets pension later.

quiz.question: Atal Pension Yojana focuses on…
options: Small regular savings that build a pension for many informal workers
One-time cash gifts with no pension

title: National Pension System
one_liner: People can save money regularly for retirement.
what: A long-term retirement savings scheme.
why: To encourage people to plan financially for future.
example: Employees invest monthly and build retirement fund.

quiz.question: NPS is mainly…
options: A voluntary, long-term pension and retirement savings system
A scheme that bans mutual funds

title: Stand-Up India
one_liner: Loans for women and SC/ST entrepreneurs.
what: A scheme encouraging underrepresented entrepreneurs.
why: Many people lacked financial support to start businesses.
example: A woman opens her own bakery using government loan.

quiz.question: Stand-Up India is designed to support…
options: Entrepreneurship among women and SC/ST communities with bank loans

title: Production Linked Incentive Scheme
one_liner: Government rewards companies for manufacturing more in India.
what: A scheme giving incentives to boost manufacturing.
why: India wanted more factories and jobs.
example: Phone companies get benefits for making devices in India.

quiz.question: PLI schemes mainly…
options: Offer incentives tied to production to grow manufacturing in India

title: Startup India Seed Fund
one_liner: Startups receive early financial support.
what: Government funding support for new startups.
why: Many startups failed due to lack of initial funding.
example: A student startup gets funds to build an app.

quiz.question: Startup India Seed Fund is meant to…
options: Help early-stage startups with funding and support

title: SWAYAM
one_liner: Students can study online courses for free.
what: A government platform offering online learning courses.
why: Many students could not access quality education easily.
example: A student learns coding online from home.

quiz.question: SWAYAM mainly helps students by...
options: Offering free online courses from anywhere
Giving free smartphones to every student

title: PM eVIDYA
one_liner: Education reached students through TV and digital platforms.
what: A program providing digital and TV-based learning.
why: Students struggled during school closures.
example: Students watch educational classes on TV channels.

quiz.question: PM eVIDYA is best known for...

title: DIKSHA
one_liner: Teachers and students got digital learning resources.
what: An online platform with educational materials and training.
why: Teachers needed better digital tools.
example: A teacher downloads lesson plans from the app.

quiz.question: DIKSHA mostly provides...
options: Digital learning resources and teacher support

title: NIPUN Bharat
one_liner: Young children focus on reading and math basics.
what: A mission to improve foundational literacy and numeracy.
why: Many children struggled with basic reading and math.
example: Kids learn reading skills in early grades.

quiz.question: NIPUN Bharat focuses on...
options: Basic reading and math in early classes

title: Vidyanjali
one_liner: Volunteers help improve schools and student learning.
what: A program connecting volunteers with schools.
why: Schools needed extra support and resources.
example: An engineer teaches students basic robotics.

title: SWAYAM PRABHA
one_liner: Educational TV channels provide free learning content.
what: A group of TV channels broadcasting educational programs.
why: Not every student had internet access.
example: Students watch science lessons on TV.
before: Education mostly internet or classroom based

title: National Digital Library
one_liner: Students can access thousands of books online.
what: A digital library with educational content.
why: Books and study materials were not easily accessible everywhere.
example: A student reads exam preparation books online.

quiz.question: National Digital Library mainly offers...
options: Online access to books and study resources

title: Atal Tinkering Labs
one_liner: Students learn innovation through hands-on experiments.
what: School labs promoting robotics, coding, and innovation.
why: Students needed practical and creative learning.
example: Students build small robots in school labs.

quiz.question: Atal Tinkering Labs encourage...
options: Hands-on innovation like robotics and coding

title: Skill Hub Initiative
one_liner: Students learn job-ready practical skills.
what: A program focused on vocational and practical training.
why: Students often lacked employable skills.
example: Students learn graphic design or coding.

title: Eklavya Model Residential Schools
one_liner: Tribal students get better residential education facilities.
what: Residential schools mainly for tribal communities.
why: Many tribal students lacked quality education access.
example: Students study and live in modern school campuses.
before: Limited educational support in tribal areas

quiz.question: Eklavya Model Residential Schools are primarily for...
options: Improving educational access for tribal students

title: National Digital Health Mission
one_liner: Health records are becoming digital and easier to access.
what: A program creating digital health IDs and online medical records.
why: Medical records were scattered and difficult to manage.
example: A patient accesses reports online through health ID.

quiz.question: National Digital Health Mission mainly enables...
options: Digital health IDs and easier medical record access

title: Pradhan Mantri Jan Aushadhi Yojana
one_liner: Affordable medicines became available to more people.
what: A scheme providing low-cost generic medicines.
why: Many people struggled to afford expensive medicines.
example: Patients buy cheaper medicines from Jan Aushadhi stores.

title: Fit India Movement
one_liner: India encouraged people to stay active and healthy.
what: A nationwide fitness and wellness campaign.
why: Lifestyle diseases and inactivity were increasing.
example: Schools organize fitness activities for students.

quiz.question: Fit India Movement mainly promotes...
options: Daily fitness and healthy lifestyle habits

title: Eat Right India
one_liner: People are encouraged to eat healthier and safer food.
what: A campaign promoting healthy eating habits and food safety.
why: Unhealthy eating habits were increasing health problems.
example: Restaurants display hygiene ratings for customers.
before: Less focus on nutrition and hygiene

quiz.question: Eat Right India is about...
options: Healthy eating and safer food practices

title: POSHAN Abhiyaan
one_liner: India focused on reducing malnutrition in children and mothers.
what: A nutrition mission improving health of women and children.
why: Malnutrition rates were high in many areas.
example: Children receive nutrition support in anganwadis.

title: TB Mukt Bharat
one_liner: India aims to eliminate tuberculosis faster.
what: A campaign to detect and treat TB patients.
why: Tuberculosis affected millions across the country.
example: Free TB testing and medicines provided to patients.

quiz.question: TB Mukt Bharat is focused on...
options: Eliminating tuberculosis through better detection and treatment

title: Anaemia Mukt Bharat
one_liner: India worked to reduce anaemia among women and children.
what: A mission to reduce iron deficiency and anaemia.
why: Many women and children suffered from weakness due to anaemia.
example: Iron tablets distributed in schools and health centers.

quiz.question: Anaemia Mukt Bharat mainly supports...
options: Iron and nutrition support to reduce anaemia

title: eSanjeevani
one_liner: People can consult doctors online from home.
what: A government telemedicine platform for online doctor consultations.
why: Many people lacked easy access to hospitals and doctors.
example: A patient talks to a doctor through video call.

title: National Health Mission
one_liner: Healthcare services improved in rural and urban areas.
what: A mission strengthening public healthcare systems.
why: Many regions lacked proper healthcare facilities.
example: More health centers and medical staff added in villages.

quiz.question: National Health Mission is mainly about...

title: Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan
one_liner: Pregnant women receive free health checkups and care.
what: A program providing safe pregnancy care for women.
why: Many pregnant women lacked proper medical support.
example: Women receive free pregnancy checkups at hospitals.

quiz.question: This scheme mainly helps with...
options: Free maternal checkups and pregnancy care

title: PM Fasal Bima Yojana
one_liner: Farmers get insurance support when crops are damaged.
what: A crop insurance scheme protecting farmers from losses.
why: Farmers faced heavy losses due to floods, droughts, and disasters.
example: A farmer receives compensation after crop damage from heavy rain.

quiz.question: PM Fasal Bima Yojana mainly gives farmers...

title: Paramparagat Krishi Vikas Yojana
one_liner: Farmers are encouraged to use organic farming methods.
what: A scheme promoting chemical-free organic farming.
why: Excessive chemical use harmed soil and health.
example: Farmers grow vegetables using natural fertilizers.

title: PM Krishi Sinchai Yojana
one_liner: More farms got access to proper irrigation.
what: A scheme improving water supply for agriculture.
why: Many farms depended only on rainfall.
example: Farmers use drip irrigation systems for crops.

quiz.question: PM Krishi Sinchai Yojana is mainly about...

title: Kisan Credit Card
one_liner: Farmers can get loans quickly for farming needs.
what: A credit scheme helping farmers access low-interest loans.
why: Farmers often borrowed money from private lenders at high interest.
example: A farmer buys seeds and fertilizer using KCC loan.

quiz.question: Kisan Credit Card helps farmers by providing...

title: Operation Greens
one_liner: Government works to stabilize vegetable prices for farmers and consumers.
what: A scheme focused on tomato, onion, and potato supply management.
why: Prices of vegetables changed drastically due to supply issues.
example: Farmers receive support during price crashes.

title: Agriculture Infrastructure Fund
one_liner: Money is provided to improve farming infrastructure.
what: A fund supporting storage, warehouses, and farm infrastructure.
why: Farmers lacked proper storage and transport facilities.
example: A warehouse is built to store crops safely.

quiz.question: This fund is used for...
options: Farm infrastructure like storage and warehouses

title: National Beekeeping and Honey Mission
one_liner: Farmers earn extra income through honey production.
what: A scheme promoting beekeeping and honey farming.
why: Farmers needed additional income opportunities.
example: A farmer starts honey production with government support.

quiz.question: National Beekeeping and Honey Mission supports...
options: Honey production and additional farm income

title: National Agriculture Market Reform
one_liner: Farmers can sell crops in wider markets more easily.
what: A reform improving digital agricultural trading systems.
why: Farmers had limited access to buyers and markets.
example: Farmers compare crop prices online before selling.

quiz.question: National Agriculture Market reform helps farmers by...
options: Expanding digital market access and price visibility

title: Rashtriya Gokul Mission
one_liner: India works to improve cattle quality and milk production.
what: A scheme supporting cattle breeding and dairy farming.
why: Farmers needed better livestock productivity.
example: Farmers receive support for improved cattle breeds.

quiz.question: Rashtriya Gokul Mission is mainly for...
options: Improving cattle breeds and dairy output

title: Sub Mission on Agricultural Mechanization
one_liner: Farmers get support to use modern farming machines.
what: A scheme encouraging farm mechanization with equipment support.
why: Manual farming was time-consuming and difficult.
example: Farmers buy tractors and harvesters with subsidies.

title: Startup India
one_liner: India encouraged young people to build startups and create jobs.
what: A program supporting startups with funding and easier rules.
why: Many young entrepreneurs struggled to start businesses.
example: A student launches a tech startup with government support.

title: Make in India
one_liner: India promoted manufacturing to create more jobs.
what: A campaign encouraging companies to manufacture in India.
why: India wanted more factories and employment opportunities.
example: Phone companies open factories in India.

title: PM Kaushal Vikas Yojana
one_liner: Youth receive free training for job-ready skills.
what: A skill training program for different industries.
why: Many young people lacked employable skills.
example: Students learn digital marketing or electrician work.

quiz.question: PMKVY is best known for...
options: Free vocational skill training for youth

title: Stand-Up India
one_liner: Women and SC/ST entrepreneurs receive business support.
what: A scheme offering loans to underrepresented entrepreneurs.
why: Many people lacked access to business funding.
example: A woman starts a clothing business with a government loan.

quiz.question: This Stand-Up India entry focuses on...
options: Loans for women and SC/ST entrepreneurs

title: Atmanirbhar Bharat Rozgar Yojana
one_liner: Businesses received support to hire more workers.
what: A scheme encouraging companies to create jobs after COVID-19.
why: Many people lost jobs during the pandemic.
example: A company hires new employees with government support.

quiz.question: Atmanirbhar Bharat Rozgar Yojana mainly helped...

title: Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Grameen Kaushalya Yojana
one_liner: Rural youth receive training for jobs and careers.
what: A rural skill development and employment program.
why: Many rural youth lacked job opportunities.
example: Village students get hospitality and IT training.

title: Production Linked Incentive Scheme
one_liner: Manufacturing growth created more employment opportunities.
what: A scheme rewarding companies for increasing production in India.
why: India wanted more factories and job creation.
example: Electronics factories hire more workers.

quiz.question: PLI (jobs view) contributes to employment by...
options: Incentivizing production growth that adds factory jobs
Replacing workers with no transition plans

title: Garib Kalyan Rojgar Abhiyan
one_liner: Migrant workers received temporary employment support.
what: A program providing jobs in rural areas during COVID-19.
why: Many migrant workers lost jobs and returned to villages.
example: Workers get employment in village infrastructure projects.

quiz.question: Garib Kalyan Rojgar Abhiyan was launched mainly for...
options: Temporary rural jobs during the COVID crisis

title: India BPO Promotion Scheme
one_liner: More IT and call center jobs were created in smaller cities.
what: A scheme promoting BPO and IT jobs outside metro cities.
why: Most tech jobs were concentrated in big cities.
example: A call center opens in a tier-2 city creating jobs.
before: IT jobs mostly in metro cities

quiz.question: India BPO Promotion Scheme aims to...
options: Expand BPO/IT jobs beyond major metro cities

title: National Apprenticeship Promotion Scheme
one_liner: Students gain real work experience while learning.
what: A scheme supporting apprenticeships and practical training.
why: Students lacked industry experience after studies.
example: A student trains in a company before full-time employment.

title: Unified Payments Interface (UPI)
one_liner: India turned smartphones into digital wallets.
what: A real-time digital payment system linked to bank accounts.
why: Online money transfers were slow and complicated.
example: A student scans a QR code to pay for food instantly.

quiz.question: UPI made payments easier by enabling...

title: CoWIN Platform
one_liner: Vaccination booking became digital and organized.
what: An online platform for vaccine registration and certificates.
why: Managing millions of vaccine appointments manually was difficult.
example: People booked COVID vaccines online using mobile phones.

quiz.question: CoWIN was mainly used for...
options: Online vaccine booking and certificate management

title: UMANG App
one_liner: Many government services became available in one app.
what: A mobile app providing access to government services.
why: People had to visit multiple websites and offices earlier.
example: Users check PF balance and pay bills through one app.

quiz.question: UMANG helps users by offering...
options: Many government services in one app

title: Aadhaar Enabled Payment System
one_liner: People can access banking services using Aadhaar.
what: A payment system using Aadhaar authentication.
why: Many rural users lacked easy banking access.
example: Villagers withdraw money using fingerprint authentication.

title: Government e-Marketplace (GeM)
one_liner: Government purchases became more digital and transparent.
what: An online platform for government procurement.
why: Traditional procurement processes were slow and less transparent.
example: Small businesses sell products directly to government departments.

title: Open Network for Digital Commerce (ONDC)
one_liner: Small businesses can compete more easily online.
what: An open digital commerce network connecting buyers and sellers.
why: Large e-commerce platforms dominated online shopping.
example: A local shop lists products on multiple apps through ONDC.

quiz.question: ONDC is designed to...
options: Create an open network for online buying/selling

title: MyGov Platform
one_liner: Citizens can directly share ideas with the government online.
what: A digital platform for public participation and feedback.
why: People had limited direct interaction with government initiatives.
example: Citizens participate in online surveys and campaigns.

title: National Digital Communications Policy
one_liner: India focused on improving internet and communication systems.
what: A policy promoting better telecom and internet connectivity.
why: Digital growth required stronger communication infrastructure.
example: Expansion of high-speed internet services in more areas.

title: Cyber Surakshit Bharat
one_liner: India increased focus on cybersecurity awareness.
what: A program promoting cyber safety and digital security.
why: Cyber attacks and online fraud cases were increasing.
example: Government employees receive cybersecurity training.

quiz.question: Cyber Surakshit Bharat focuses on...
options: Cybersecurity awareness and safer digital practices

title: National Digital Literacy Mission
one_liner: More people learned basic digital and computer skills.
what: A mission teaching digital literacy across India.
why: Many people lacked basic technology knowledge.
example: Villagers learn how to use smartphones and online services.

quiz.question: National Digital Literacy Mission aims to...
options: Teach basic digital skills to more citizens
